text: On intraoperative endoscopy, an inflamed area in the fundus of the stomach could be identified.
With insufflation, there appeared to be a leak in that same area as denoted by the arrow.
The operation began with our standardized laparoscopic setup for a foregut procedure.
There were many adhesions intra-abdominally, likely from the previous Nissen fundoplication.
Using a combination of ultrasonic shears and laparoscopic scissors, the adhesions were carefully dissected off of the liver.
The hiatus was finally able to be dissected free, giving us full visualization into the mediastinum.
The fundoplication itself was then carefully dissected and incised sharply, taking great care not to create any full thickness gastronomies.
Next the anatomic left side of the hiatus was entered.
The mucosa of the fistula can be seen here.
Upon initial dissection we could clearly see that the defect was much larger than had been indicated on both the CT scan and on endoscopy.
Here we can see the grasper retracting the gastric mucosa and the suction irrigator debriding the left pleura.
As we can clearly see, the previous empyema as well as the scar tissue from the decortication made the dissection on the anatomic left side extremely difficult.
In order to find a clear plane to work with, we switched over our dissection to the anatomic right side.
Although there were still adhesions from her previous nissen fundoplication, this scar tissue was much more favorable to work with.
The retroesophageal window was eventually achieved and the dissection was further carried out toward the anatomic left side.
The medial edge of the fistula could be visualized here.
The fundoplication was further dissected free and the wrap was undone.
This was performed in order to restore proper anatomy.
Once the fundoplication was fully dissected free, it was pushed posteriorly to restore it to the anatomic left side.
The full extent of the fistula could finally be analyzed.
The defect measured more than five centimeters long, too large to simply over-sew.
The decision was made to resect this area in order to provide a clean and healthy tissue plane for proper healing.
To obtain proper resection of the area, retraction sutures were placed at both the inferior and the superior portion of the gastrotomy.
A third suture was also placed in the middle of the defect in order to ensure full thickness resection of the fistula.
Three firings of the laparoscopic GIA stapler were used to resect the fistula.
The staple line was then over-sewn with a running permanent V-lock suture.
This was done in order to bury the staple line as well as to ensure hemostasis.
A drain was then placed along the staple line.
This was done in order to potentially capture both a leak from the staple line as well as to drain the left chest.
